chapters.title: Essentialism means minimizing triviality and focusing on things that hold significance
chapters.text:
Our lives are so full of tasks and responsibilities that we struggle to set our priorities straight; we often find it challenging to identify which are the most important to us.

Even though we go through our to-do list and pick the ones we should prioritize, we still have too much on our hands. These excessive activities massively hinder our productivity.

Fortunately, by adopting essentialism’s four main points and focusing on only a few essential tasks, you can set your priorities straight.

• Do less but do it better. The basic principle of essentialism is recognizing and discarding the less important things in your life. After doing this, you can focus your attention on what matters.
• Toss the idea that you need to accomplish everything and choose specific directions from which you can excel.
• Dedicate time to one or two most important things instead of trying to make little progress in many areas.
• Question yourself regularly and update your plans subsequently. Determining what’s worth your time and what you should let go of is continuous.

Less but better is the key motto of essentialism.

You should always decide whether what you're doing is worth it or if you should invest your time and energy in another area. Ultimately, once those few essential tasks stand out from the trivial many, an essentialist wastes no time implementing the changes.

Dive into this summary to uncover the secrets and benefits of essentialism and how you can use these principles to better your life.

chapters.title: If we let ourselves become swamped with tasks, we lose the ability to make choices for ourselves
chapters.text:
Do you always say ‘’I have to” instead of “I choose to”? If so, then you are living a non-essentialist lifestyle. So many of us fail to take charge of our ability to choose. This problem happens because of learned helplessness. We've grown so familiar with the feeling of being overwhelmed that we approach our lives with utmost passivity. If you start viewing your life as a compilation of choices, not obligations, you’ll begin to realize your true potential.

The term learned helplessness originates from a two-part experiment on three groups of dogs exposed to electric shocks. In the first part of the trial, the dogs in group one had a lever that would stop the shocks; group two had a lever with no effect; group three received no shocks at all.

In the second part of the study, all the dogs were put in a huge box that was divided in two: one half emitted electric shocks, and the other released none. Those dogs from groups one and three made their way to the shock-free side. However, the dogs from group two remained in the shock zone. Simply put, they had learned to be helpless.

If you give up your power to choose, you permit others to choose for you.

When people forget about their free will, they get used to helplessness; their lives become a function of other people’s choices. Such is the way of the non-essentialist. On the other hand, essentialists embrace the power of choice, which is the focus of their belief. No matter the situation they find themselves in, essentialists always remember that they can make choices — they never give up their right to choose. They understand their potential for change and impact. This power motivates them to move forward and stay focused. No matter the obstacle, they believe in their abilities to overcome it. Nothing can truly hold them back. They are similar to stoics in this way — they understand their power to live their lives to the fullest.

chapters.title: Be open to the idea of doing less and see trade-offs as a part of life
chapters.text:
If time travel was possible and you could go back and build wealth by investing in a company, which one would you choose? IBM? Microsoft? Apple? While the enormous success these companies have enjoyed over the years might make them an obvious pick for some, Southwest Airlines would have given you the most significant return on your investment. The truth is Southwest Airlines displayed a remarkable level of success by focusing on one of the key principles of essentialism: doing only a few important things very well.

Instead of offering their customers additional choices, like first-class seating, meals, and seat reservations, Southwest Airlines focused on one thing: flying people from one point to another, and that’s it — no frills necessary. They realized that they would surely fail if they tried to do everything. Instead, they focused on excellently doing only a few things, which helped them become successful. However, adopting this approach can prove difficult, as it requires us to be willing and able to make trade-offs.

While it might sound easy enough to cut out unnecessary tasks and leave only the most important, we become convinced that we can do it all. For instance, when the success of Southwest Airlines became obvious, Continental Airlines decided to copy its strategy. Rather than cut back to a few vital essentials, Continental erroneously concluded they could do it all. Their solution was to continue running their regular airline and create a separate brand, Continental Lite, to provide a budget service.

However, the operational inefficiencies that resulted from pursuing both strategies meant that Continental Lite wasn’t price competitive. Ultimately, they lost millions because they couldn’t let go of the non-essential and concentrate on what was vital.

Any organization set up for long-term success must embrace the reality of trade-offs — prioritizing one strategy over another.

Now that you have a clear concept of how far people often deviate from the path of essentialism, let's discover what we can do to retrace our steps and return to what matters most.

chapters.title: Distance yourself to learn to discern between the essential and the trivial
chapters.text:
It's difficult for anyone to be bored these days. Modern technologies give us access to a wealth of communication and entertainment at any given second. And while such constant fun sounds excellent on paper, it’s important to remember that boredom can actually be good for you. It gives you much-needed time to be in a state akin to meditation when you can converse with yourself. A period in which you have nothing to do can give you a chance to think clearly about what you have to get done. Planning your tasks and managing the time you need to spend on them is key to success. Structure eliminates chaos, bringing productivity and efficiency to your life. This habit also reduces stress and confusion. To make sure you have that time, clear a break in your schedule daily to give yourself space to escape and think.

In fact, some of the greatest minds in history, such as Sir Isaac Newton and Albert Einstein, used this invaluable technique. Both sometimes confined themselves in isolation to escape, allowing themselves to think about their groundbreaking theories. The same goes for most successful CEOs today — they schedule a few hours of “blank space” in their calendar every day to do some thinking.

However, escaping isn’t just a way to separate essential responsibilities from the rest; you can also use it to remain focused on the big picture.

Most times, people get so lost in the petty, day-to-day tasks that they forget the reason they are doing those things in the first place.

Essentialism teaches us to focus on what's relevant by always keeping the bigger picture in mind. One way to achieve this is by keeping a journal, but instead of scribbling down everything you experience, force yourself to write as little as possible. This approach will make you think through everything you’ve done and sift out only what you deem essential. And as you read back through your notes, you will see the bigger picture emerging.

chapters.title: Boost your creativity by playing, and make sure you take some time to rest
chapters.text:
Unfortunately, having decided that play is trivial and unproductive, adults tend to have a clear-cut boundary between work and play. We believe the latter is there purely for entertainment and doesn’t help us to advance our goals. Simply put, it’s a waste of time. However, the essentialist recognizes that play is an indispensable tool for inspiration. It makes way for creative and innovative ideas and encourages an artistic approach. Play also makes you feel better, allowing you to have quality fun.

If you want to find out what’s essential in your life, you can free your mind by playing so that you can approach any issue creatively.

Here is how play relates to creativity:

• It helps us form new connections between ideas we would never have considered.
• It is a remedy for stress, one of the principal factors in unproductivity.
• It helps us prioritize our responsibilities.

We see the value of play exhibited in the culture of companies like Twitter, Pixar, and Google. They promote play among their employees by offering improv comedy classes or decorating the office with fun and unconventional figurines and knick-knacks. The reason why they do this? Because they know that a playful employee is an inspired and productive one.

But play, as important as it is, should never take priority over good rest and sleep. Non-essentialists consider sleep and play the same thing — they see them as luxuries, wastes of potentially productive hours. But this approach is entirely backward, as sleep enhances your ability to think and connect ideas and maximizes your productivity during your active hours. A proper sleep schedule is essential to your physical and mental well-being. The more rest you get, the more productive you are the following day.

Did you know? Research at Harvard Medical School found that going 24 hours without sleep or getting a weekly average of about five hours of sleep per night results in a cognitive impairment equal to a 0.1 percent blood alcohol level.

chapters.title: Be ready to keep emotions under control when cutting out non-essential things
chapters.text:
We frequently think that all of our tasks and responsibilities are essential in one way or another. You’ve probably gone through something similar when organizing your closet. You begin with the mindset that: “If I never wear it, I must get rid of it.” But you soon start making exceptions, telling yourself things like: “I know I never wear this howling-wolf shirt, but I might one day!” So, you decide to keep it, and your closet remains as jam-packed as ever. It is a never-ending cycle that’s difficult to escape. Fortunately, there are ways to do it.

So, how can you avoid making this mistake? In short, be very strict with your criteria. To do this, try using the 90-percent rule. Start by considering the most relevant standard for your decision. For instance, if you’re clearing out your closet, that criteria might be, “Do I still need this?” Then score it on a scale of 0 to 100. With the 90-percent rule, anything below 90 (even an 89) is a zero. After weighing all the options, abandon everything that scored less than 90.

Essentialists dedicate much of their efforts to eliminating obstacles and simplifying the work to the best of their abilities.

Another approach is to conclude, “If it isn’t a clear yes, then it’s a clear no.” An easy way to utilize this is to list the three minimum things that something must have for you to keep it and three ideal criteria that you expect it to meet. Then when making the decision, something must pass three minimum requirements and at least two ideal ones.

Thinking this way will help you avoid trivial matters slipping through. For instance, there is no way that the howling-wolf shirt in your closet would pass the three minimum requirements:

• Is it stylish?
• Would I wear it every day?
• Will anyone laugh at me for wearing it?

chapters.title: Say no to non-essential tasks and plan the essential ones carefully
chapters.text:
What goes after listing everything you need to do away with? It's easy to throw away a shirt you never wear, but these decisions get complicated when other people are involved.

The right “no” said at the right time can alter the course of history. ~ Greg McKeown

We tend to find saying “no” very difficult, feeling socially awkward and pressured not to disappoint the people around us. We are concerned that saying “no” might impair our relationships. However, we need to say “no” more often and reserve “yes” only for the things that genuinely matter. To be able to do this, you’ll need to treat the decision and the relationship as separate entities.

The pain of saying “no” may cause regret for around ten minutes as you worry about someone else’s disappointment or that you’re missing out on something, but you might regret saying “yes” for much longer.

Failing to say “no” to the things that aren’t essential can cause you to miss out on great opportunities.

Once you're used to saying “no” when it’s in your best interests, you can concentrate on planning the remaining vital tasks. Become clear on your goals by having an essential intent: one primary objective that is both concrete and inspirational. Imagine that your goal is to end world hunger. Though very inspirational, this goal is not concrete and thus fails as your essential intent. Trying to follow an objective of this size will become unruly due to its vagueness. Think about this goal: build 150 affordable, environmentally friendly, storm-resistant homes for families. This goal is specific, concrete, and also inspirational.

One way to ensure your plan is clear is by asking yourself: “How will I know when I’ve reached my goal?” If you can give a reasonable answer to this question, then you have clarity about what you are doing.

chapters.title: Withdrawing from failures and setting boundaries will stop you from doing unnecessary things
chapters.text:
Have you ever done something you were sure was a waste of energy and time just because you committed to it? Many people fall into this sunk-cost bias — the tendency to keep investing money, time, and energy into something we already know is not likely to succeed. Unluckily, every small investment makes it more difficult to let go while concurrently increasing the amount we are sure to lose.

To exemplify the sunk-cost bias, consider the dramatic failure of the Concorde jet. Although it was an extraordinary engineering achievement, the cost made it worthless and doomed to commercial failure. Despite the glaring facts, the British and French governments fell into a sunk-cost bias and continued funding it for 40 years, fully aware that they would never get most of their money back.

Only when we admit we have made a mistake in committing to something can we make a mistake a part of our past. ~ Greg McKeown

You can easily avoid this trap by developing the courage to admit your flaws and mistakes and let them go. Besides, you can avoid this whole situation by setting clear boundaries. While non-essentialists think boundaries are unnecessary constraints, they are liberating on a fundamental level.

For instance, imagine a schoolyard on a busy street: at this school, children are only allowed to play in a small part of the yard next to the school buildings, and the teachers have to watch them to ensure they stay within the boundary. But what if a fence clearly defined where the children could safely be? Then the teachers could do something better with their time since they wouldn’t have to be so concerned about children being near traffic, and the kids could play freely and safely.

Boundaries exist to make your life easier and more enjoyable, not to constrain you. For instance, you could set clear boundaries between work and family. If your children aren’t allowed in the office, then work shouldn’t be allowed in your home.

If it’s evident that something isn’t going to work out, do not hesitate to cut your losses and abandon ship.

chapters.title: The best way to maintain the essentials is by preparing carefully and eliminating what slows you down
chapters.text:
Once you’ve started following the principles of essentialism, it’s time to commit to the last step: execution. Becoming an essentialist requires that you not only identify what’s slowing you down and find ways to work around it but also to eliminate it.

For instance, imagine you’re a boy scout leader who must get your troop to camp before sunset. To keep things fair, each scout carries equal provisions in their backpack. But, there is a problem: some scouts are much faster than others, so the group spreads out, and the kids in the back risk getting left behind. Your first (non-essential) solution is to make stops now and then so that the slower ones can catch up. Next, you try moving the kids from the back to the front, so everyone stays together, but this slows everyone down.

Finally, you see the essentialist solution: take away some of the provisions in the slower kids’ backpacks and put them in the faster kids’ backpacks. You’ve eliminated the problem by thinking with the principles of essentialism!

The essentialist produces more by removing more, not by doing more.

You can prevent unnecessary hindrances by being prepared. One of our biggest mistakes is assuming that our plans will go as expected. However, an essentialist does not think this way. Instead, they make the proper preparations assuming that things might go wrong. Always be prepared for many potential scenarios and be ready to deal with the outcomes but be careful not to delve into complete pessimism. Recognize that your plans cannot always go as expected, but remember that you can fix the outcomes you don’t like.

Whatever you do, whether it’s delivering a presentation at work or taking your kids to school, always give yourself a buffer of 50% of the time you assume it will take. This way, you can always leave room to fix anything slowing you down.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
We are so swamped with tasks and responsibilities that we find it difficult to set our priorities straight. Identifying which of the tasks on our to-do lists are the most important to us is downright tedious. Even if we go through the list of tasks and pick the ones we should prioritize, we still have too much on our hands. These excessive activities massively hinder our productivity. Fortunately, adopting essentialism can help us pinpoint what is most important to us and set our priorities straight.

Regardless of how it might seem, only a few things are essential to our goals and well-being, and everything else is unimportant. Concentrating on these few vital things and learning to do better by doing less can create a far more productive and fulfilling life. So stop thinking that you need to accomplish everything, and choose specific directions in which you can excel. Focus on a particular area and make giant strides in the things that matter most to you, rather than stretching yourself thin by trying to make little progress in many areas.

Finally, make it a habit always to question yourself and update your plans. The process of determining what’s essential and what's trivial is continuous. You can't just nod to the principle of essentialism now and then; you have to practice it regularly in a disciplined manner. When you start seeing essentialism as a way of life, it becomes the essence of who you are.

Try this

Instead of constantly adding more and more duties and material possessions to your life, try to find ways to cut things out. Start with the place where you live. Decide on the things that are not essential. If you don’t need them, throw them out.
